real_meaning: ～てもいい expresses permission — either granting it ('you may do X') or asking for it ('is it okay if I do X?'). The core feeling is 'even if X happens, it's fine/acceptable,' so it's softer and more permissive than a direct command. In conversation, the question form ～てもいいですか is the polite, go-to way to ask if something is allowed without sounding pushy.

formation.rule:
Attach もいい to the て-form of a verb. For い-adjectives, drop い and add くてもいい. For な-adjectives and nouns, add でもいい.
• Verb: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてもいい
• い-adj: <ruby>安<rt>やす</rt></ruby>い → <ruby>安<rt>やす</rt></ruby>くてもいい
• な-adj: <ruby>静<rt>しず</rt></ruby>かだ → <ruby>静<rt>しず</rt></ruby>かでもいい
• Noun: <ruby>学<rt>がく</rt></ruby><ruby>生<rt>せい</rt></ruby>だ → <ruby>学<rt>がく</rt></ruby><ruby>生<rt>せい</rt></ruby>でもいい
formation.common_mistake: Using the dictionary form instead of the て-form. ✗ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるもいい → ✓ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてもいい

sentences.jp: <ruby>先<rt>せん</rt></ruby><ruby>生<rt>せい</rt></ruby>、トイレ<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>ってもいいですか？
sentences.translation: Teacher, may I go to the bathroom?
sentences.notes: The classic permission-request form — note the て-form contracts naturally to <ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>って in speech, which is standard even in polite registers.

sentences.jp: もう<ruby>帰<rt>かえ</rt></ruby>ってもいいよ。お<ruby>疲<rt>つか</rt></ruby>れ。
sentences.translation: You can head home now. Good work.
sentences.notes: Here the speaker is granting permission rather than asking for it — よ at the end softens it into a warm, reassuring statement rather than a command.

sentences.jp: ご<ruby>不<rt>ふ</rt></ruby><ruby>明<rt>めい</rt></ruby>な<ruby>点<rt>てん</rt></ruby>がございましたら、いつでもお<ruby>問<rt>と</rt></ruby>い<ruby>合<rt>あ</rt></ruby>わせいただいてもよろしいでしょうか。
sentences.translation: If you have any questions, would it be alright to contact us at any time?
sentences.notes: In formal/business Japanese, もいい upgrades to もよろしい, and the whole phrasing becomes more deferential — learners should recognize this as the same structure dressed up for keigo.

sentences.jp: べつに<ruby>来<rt>こ</rt></ruby>なくてもいいけど。
sentences.translation: You don't have to come, but… (it's up to you)
sentences.notes: The negative form ～なくてもいい ('you don't have to') expresses that something is not required — a completely different nuance that surprises learners who expect permission only.

confused_with.contrast: Both express permission, but ～てもいい is warm and everyday, while ～てもかまわない literally means 'I don't mind if...' — it's more detached or indifferent. Compare: <ruby>写<rt>しゃ</rt></ruby><ruby>真<rt>しん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>撮<rt>と</rt></ruby>ってもいいですよ ('Yes, please go ahead and take a photo!') vs. <ruby>写<rt>しゃ</rt></ruby><ruby>真<rt>しん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>撮<rt>と</rt></ruby>ってもかまいませんよ ('I don't object to you taking a photo'). The かまわない version signals mild indifference rather than genuine welcome. Use ～てもいい when you want to sound friendly and permissive; use ～てもかまわない when something is technically fine but you're not especially enthusiastic.

confusion_set.use_when: Granting or asking for permission in a warm, everyday way — 'it's okay to do X / may I do X?'
Saying something is not necessary or not required — 'you don't have to do X'
confusion_set.example: <ruby>全<rt>ぜん</rt></ruby><ruby>部<rt>ぶ</rt></ruby><ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べなくてもいいよ。

confusion_set.use_when: Expressing prohibition — 'you must not do X'; the direct opposite of ～てもいい
confusion_set.example: ここで<ruby>写<rt>しゃ</rt></ruby><ruby>真<rt>しん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>撮<rt>と</rt></ruby>ってはいけません。

bunpro_tip: Think of も as 'even' — 'even if you do X, it's fine (いい).' This helps you remember both the て-form requirement and the permissive nuance. When BunPro gives you a cue about asking permission, mentally check: am I forming the て-form first, or did I accidentally use the dictionary form?
